model_narrative: We plan production for a small workshop across two seasons. In each season we decide how many units of each product to make on each machine under either a normal or an overtime shift, how many units of each product to sell, and how many to carry over in storage to the next season. Making units costs money that depends on the machine, product, period, and shift, and carrying units in storage costs money per unit held. Selling units brings in revenue at a per-unit price that varies by product and season. The objective is to maximize the total profit, which is the sales revenue earned over the horizon less the total production and storage cost.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the planning periods in chronological order; summer is the opening period and winter follows it, so any stock stored at the end of summer carries forward into winter

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the production modes available, namely a normal shift and an overtime shift

sets.name: k
sets.doc: the products that can be made, sold, and stored

sets.name: l
sets.doc: the machines that perform production

params.name: t
params.doc: the machine time needed to make one unit of a product on a given machine in a given period and mode, in hours per unit

params.name: a
params.doc: the machine hours available on a machine in a given period and mode, in hours

params.name: c
params.doc: the production cost to make one unit of a product on a given machine in a given period and mode, in dollars per unit

params.name: s
params.doc: the storage cost charged for each unit of a product held from one period to the next, in dollars per unit

params.name: p
params.doc: the selling price per unit of a product in a given period, in dollars per unit

params.name: mh
params.doc: the base machine hours per unit for making a product on a machine; a value of zero marks a machine-product pair that is impossible because that machine cannot make that product, while any nonzero value marks a feasible pairing

params.name: d
params.doc: the demand for each product in each period, in units; this is enforced elsewhere as a lower bound on units sold and is not itself a constraint of this set

params.name: h
params.doc: the storage capacity for each product, in units; this is enforced elsewhere as an upper bound on units stored and is not itself a constraint of this set

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the number of units of a product made on a given machine in a given period and mode

vars.name: y
vars.doc: the number of units of a product stored at the end of each period

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the number of units of a product sold in each period

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: the total cost over the whole horizon, covering production and storage, in dollars

vars.name: revenue
vars.doc: the total sales revenue over the whole horizon, in dollars

vars.name: profit
vars.doc: the total profit over the whole horizon, in dollars

description: Profit is what is left after costs are taken out of sales. Set the total profit equal to the total revenue minus the total cost.

description: The total cost gathers everything spent over the whole horizon. For each product in each period, charge the storage cost for every unit of that product held over, and charge the production cost for every unit of that product made, summed over every machine and every production mode. Set the total cost equal to the sum of all these amounts across all products and all periods.

description: The total revenue comes from selling. For each product in each period, value the units sold at that product's selling price for that period, and set the total revenue equal to the sum of those amounts across all products and all periods.

description: Each machine has only so many hours available. For every combination of period, production mode, and machine, the total machine time consumed by making the products on that machine, adding up the time across all products, must not exceed the hours available on that machine in that period and mode.

description: Everything available of a product in a period is either sold or stored, and what is available is what was made that period plus what was carried in. For each product in each period, take the total units produced that period across all machines and production modes, counting only the machines that are actually able to make that product, and add the units of that product carried in from storage at the end of the previous period. In the opening period there is no previous period, so nothing is carried in. This available amount must equal the units sold that period plus the units stored at the end of that period.
